one_line_takeaway: By linking per-layer intrinsic dimensionality to on-/off-manifold adversarial examples, the paper both explains why adversarial training helps vision models and encoder-LLMs so differently and turns that insight into SMAAT, which adversarially trains encoder-LLMs at their lowest-ID last layer for ~3x cheaper AT with markedly higher robustness and unchanged clean accuracy.
key_finding: Perturbing the lowest-ID (last) layer of encoder-LLMs gives simultaneously better robustness AND scalability without sacrificing clean accuracy. On classification, SMAAT raises average robust accuracy over the best baseline by 8.6/15.7/28.8 points for BERT and 6.0/5.8/19.0 points for RoBERTa on AGNEWS/IMDB/YELP, while clean accuracy is maintained in 5 of 6 cases (worst case a 0.5-point drop). As a GCG-resistant safety filter it reaches 97-100% detection of adversarially-suffixed harmful prompts where standard and FreeLB++ filters drop to 0% on AdvBench. On RAG it drives robust Recall@100 to 73.5% under 50 poisoned passages vs 32.2% (FreeLB++) and 7.2% (standard). Training costs only ~5.2 min/epoch, essentially the same as standard (non-adversarial) training (5.1) and ~3x cheaper than FreeLB++ (15.6). The layer-selection insight also explains prior results (YOPO's first-layer emphasis for vision, TMD's last-layer AE detection for enc-LLMs, and the multi-layer LAT of Sheshadri et al.).
core_contribution: The paper makes two distinct contributions. (1) An explanatory analysis: it is the first work to explain WHY adversarial training (AT) affects model families differently, by connecting the per-layer intrinsic dimensionality (ID) of the data manifold to the on-/off-manifold nature of adversarial examples (AEs). Using the manifold conjecture, it argues that off-manifold AEs (OFM-AEs) drive robustness while on-manifold AEs (ONM-AEs) drive generalization, and empirically shows that in the FIRST layer (where classical AT injects perturbations) encoder-LLMs have high ID -> more ONM-AEs (good generalization, weak robustness), whereas vision models and decoder-LLMs have low ID -> more OFM-AEs (strong robustness, degraded generalization). (2) A method: SMAAT (Scalable Manifold-Aware Adversarial Training), which perturbs the intermediate layer with the LOWEST intrinsic dimensionality (empirically the LAST layer, l*=n, for enc-LLMs) instead of the input layer. Because AEs are generated at a late layer, only a short backward-forward PGD chain is needed, cutting AT GPU cost by 25-33% (roughly 3x faster than standard AT) while increasing robustness and preserving clean accuracy. Note: the manifold conjecture, twoNN ID estimation, LAT, and manifold-based defenses (TMD, Defense-GAN) are cited prior work, not this paper's own inventions; the paper's own novelty is the ID-based explanation and the layer-selection AT algorithm.
threat_model: Adversarial examples against encoder-based LLMs deployed in three security-relevant roles: (a) text classifiers (sentiment/topic) attacked by word-substitution adversaries -- PWWS (synonym-based), TextFooler (nearest-neighbor embedding), and BERT-Attack (masked-LM); (b) safety/content-moderation filters attacked by GCG, which appends gradient-optimized adversarial suffixes to harmful prompts to evade harmful-content detection; (c) RAG retrievers attacked by corpus-poisoning that injects adversarial passages into the retrieval corpus so malicious passages are selected into the top-k. The defender trains the model with SMAAT to make it robust to these attacks while keeping clean-task accuracy. The paper assumes white-box access for AT (gradients available) and evaluates against standard NLP-robustness attacks; it does not claim certified guarantees.
limitations: 1) SMAAT's efficiency and robustness gains are specific to encoder-LLMs: for vision models and decoder-LLMs the lowest-ID layer is the FIRST layer, so SMAAT degenerates to standard AT with no speed-up. 2) The central ID->OFM-AE claim is a hypothesis supported by a hand-wavy tangent-space argument plus correlational evidence; OFM proportion is only a PROXY (reconstruction error), not directly measured, and the manifold conjecture itself is assumed rather than proven. 3) It is not a certified defense and is evaluated only against a fixed set of known attacks (PWWS/TextFooler/BERT-Attack/GCG/corpus-poisoning); adaptive attacks targeting the frozen-lower-layers scheme are not deeply explored. 4) On individual attacks SMAAT is not always best -- e.g., RSMI beats it on PWWS for both BERT and RoBERTa on AGNEWS -- it wins on the average and on transfer/black-box-style attacks. 5) The gamma reconstruction-error threshold and layer-ID computation add a one-time per-model/per-task tuning step (claimed marginal).

datasets_used: LAT dataset of Sheshadri et al. 2024 (LLaMA-2 layer-wise AT analysis)
quantitative_results: Average robust-accuracy improvement over best baseline (BERT): +8.6 AGNEWS, +15.7 IMDB, +28.8 YELP
Average robust-accuracy improvement over best baseline (RoBERTa): +6.0 AGNEWS, +5.8 IMDB, +19.0 YELP
SMAAT average robust accuracy (AR) BERT: 73.5 AGNEWS / 67.4 IMDB / 75.9 YELP; clean acc 94.6/92.2/97.0
SMAAT average robust accuracy (AR) RoBERTa: 76.9 AGNEWS / 72.9 IMDB / 82.6 YELP; clean acc 94.6/93.5/98.0
Safety filter AUA vs GCG on AdvBench: SMAAT 100 (BERT) / 99.2 (RoBERTa) / 97.5 (DistilBERT) vs Standard and FreeLB++ = 0 for all three
Safety filter AUA on HH-RLHF: SMAAT 51.6 (BERT) / 96.8 (RoBERTa) / 39.5 (DistilBERT) vs Standard 26.4/0.3/6.5
RAG robust Recall@100 under N=50 poisoned passages: SMAAT 73.5 vs FreeLB++ 32.2 vs Standard 7.2; under N=10: SMAAT 97.1 vs FreeLB++ 60.7 vs Standard 22.2
Training time IMDB/BERT: SMAAT 5.2 min/epoch vs Standard(non-AT) 5.1, FreeLB++ 15.6, ASCC 25.7, SAFER 8.2, RSMI 15.4 (approx 3x faster than standard AT)
GPU-time reduction of AT: 25-33% of standard AT
For enc-LLMs l*=n so AE-generation cost reduces to O(P*(d_n)^2) with d_n = number of classes (<5 typical)
